sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Prepositions, conjunctions, and adverbs are essential parts of speech that connect ideas, indicate relationships, and modify verbs, adjectives, and other adverbs. This lesson covers the most important ones in French.
examples: Preposition: Je vais à Paris (I go to Paris)
Conjunction: J'aime le café et le thé (I like coffee and tea)
Adverb: Il parle lentement (He speaks slowly)

sections.title: Common Prepositions
sections.content: Prepositions indicate relationships between nouns/pronouns and other words. They often indicate location, direction, time, or manner.
examples: à (to, at): Je vais à l'école (I go to school), Je suis à la maison (I am at home)
de (of, from): Le livre de Marie (Marie's book), Je viens de Paris (I come from Paris)
en (in, to): Je suis en France (I am in France), Je vais en ville (I go to town)
dans (in, inside): Je suis dans la maison (I am inside the house), Je mets le livre dans le sac (I put the book in the bag)
sur (on, about): Le livre est sur la table (The book is on the table), Un livre sur l'histoire (A book about history)
sous (under): Le chat est sous la table (The cat is under the table)
entre (between): La bibliothèque est entre la banque et le café (The library is between the bank and the café)
pour (for): C'est pour toi (It's for you), Je travaille pour gagner de l'argent (I work to earn money)

sections.title: Prepositions with Countries and Cities
sections.content: The preposition used with countries and cities depends on the gender of the country name and whether it's a city.
examples: à + city: Je vais à Paris, à Lyon, à Marseille
en + feminine country: Je vais en France, en Espagne, en Italie
au + masculine country: Je vais au Canada, au Japon, au Brésil
aux + plural country: Je vais aux États-Unis, aux Pays-Bas
à + masculine country starting with vowel: Je vais à Cuba (but usually en Oman, en Iran)

sections.title: Common Conjunctions
sections.content: Conjunctions connect words, phrases, or clauses. They can be coordinating (connecting equal elements) or subordinating (introducing subordinate clauses).
examples: et (and): J'aime le café et le thé (I like coffee and tea)
ou (or): Tu veux du café ou du thé? (Do you want coffee or tea?)
mais (but): J'aime le café, mais je n'aime pas le thé (I like coffee, but I don't like tea)
car (because): Je suis fatigué, car j'ai travaillé toute la journée (I'm tired because I worked all day)
donc (therefore): Il pleut, donc je reste à la maison (It's raining, therefore I stay at home)
ni...ni (neither...nor): Je n'aime ni le café ni le thé (I like neither coffee nor tea)
que (that): Je crois qu'il vient (I believe that he is coming)
si (if): Si tu étudies, tu réussiras (If you study, you will succeed)

sections.title: Common Adverbs
sections.content: Adverbs modify verbs, adjectives, or other adverbs. They often end in -ment (formed from adjectives).
examples: Adverbs of manner: lentement (slowly), rapidement (quickly), bien (well), mal (badly)
Adverbs of frequency: souvent (often), rarement (rarely), toujours (always), jamais (never)
Adverbs of time: maintenant (now), hier (yesterday), demain (tomorrow), déjà (already), encore (still/again)
Adverbs of place: ici (here), là (there), partout (everywhere), nulle part (nowhere)
Adverbs of quantity: très (very), trop (too), assez (enough), peu (little)

sections.title: Forming Adverbs from Adjectives
sections.content: Many adverbs are formed by adding -ment to the feminine form of an adjective. If the adjective ends in a vowel, just add -ment.
examples: lent → lentement (slow → slowly)
rapide → rapidement (quick → quickly)
bon → bien (good → well) - irregular
facile → facilement (easy → easily)
heureux → heureux → heureuse → heureusement (happy → happily)
vrai → vraiment (true → truly) - from masculine form

sections.title: Position of Adverbs
sections.content: Adverbs can be placed before or after the verb, depending on the adverb and the tense. Short adverbs of frequency often come before the verb.
examples: Je parle souvent (I often speak) - adverb after verb
J'étudie toujours le soir (I always study in the evening)
Nous allons rarement au cinéma (We rarely go to the cinema)
Il a rapidement fini (He quickly finished) - adverb between auxiliary and past participle
Elle parle lentement (She speaks slowly) - adverb after verb

sections.title: Practice Exercise
sections.content: Write sentences using: 1) 5 different prepositions, 2) 5 different conjunctions, 3) 5 different adverbs. Then create 5 sentences that combine prepositions, conjunctions, and adverbs.
